title: Terraform is declarative desired-state
description: You describe the end state in HCL; Terraform diffs it against recorded state and computes the minimal set of API calls, not imperative steps.
body: Use when reasoning about why Terraform behaves unlike a script. Unlike imperative provisioning, HCL declares the target; Terraform builds a dependency graph, compares desired config to the state file, and emits create/update/delete actions, so order is derived from references not file position. Do not read .tf top-to-bottom as sequential steps. Actions: express dependencies via references (or depends_on) and let the graph order operations. Validation signals include a stable plan that converges to no-changes when re-run (idempotence). Risk boundary: assuming imperative ordering or running apply repeatedly to 'force' convergence hides a real cycle or provider bug — investigate non-convergent plans.
